phase1.explanation:
Multithreading allows a program to run multiple tasks simultaneously:

--- Process vs Thread ---

Process:
• An instance of a program running
• Has its own memory space
• Heavyweight
• Example: Running your IDE, browser, and game simultaneously

Thread:
• A lightweight unit of execution within a process
• Shares memory with other threads
• Lightweight (cheaper to create)
• Example: A web browser loading multiple tabs concurrently

--- Concurrency vs Parallelism ---

Concurrency:
• Multiple tasks making progress, but not necessarily at the same time
• One CPU core, tasks take turns
• Example: Single-core CPU running multiple programs

Parallelism:
• Multiple tasks running at exactly the same time
• Multiple CPU cores
• Example: Multi-core CPU running multiple programs simultaneously

--- Thread Lifecycle ---

1. NEW — Created but not started (Thread t = new Thread())
2. RUNNABLE — Ready to run (t.start())
3. RUNNING — Actually executing (CPU time)
4. BLOCKED — Waiting for a lock or I/O
5. WAITING — Waiting indefinitely for notification
6. TIMED_WAITING — Waiting for a specific time
7. TERMINATED — Finished execution

--- Why Multithreading? ---

Benefits:
• Better CPU utilization
• Responsive UI (don't freeze)
• Efficient I/O operations
• Better performance on multi-core systems

Challenges:
• Race conditions (data corruption)
• Deadlocks (threads waiting forever)
• Complexity in debugging
• Hard to reason about

--- Common Use Cases ---

• Web servers handling multiple requests
• GUI applications (UI thread + background tasks)
• Download managers (multiple files simultaneously)
• Database operations (parallel queries)
• Batch processing (split data across threads)

--- Java Thread Support ---

Java has built-in multithreading support:
• Thread class
• Runnable interface
• synchronized keyword
• wait/notify/notifyAll
• Executor framework
• java.util.concurrent package

In the next lessons, we'll learn how to create and manage threads!

phase2.explanation:
Conceptual lesson — no broken code!

Common misconception: "More threads always means better performance."

Actually: More threads can reduce performance due to:
• Context switching overhead
• Memory consumption
• Increased complexity
• Limited CPU cores

Recommendation: Number of threads ≈ Number of CPU cores (for CPU-bound tasks)

Another misconception: "Threads are completely independent and don't interact."

Actually: Threads share memory and resources. This is what causes race conditions and deadlocks!

Example of a race condition:
Two threads both increment a shared counter: counter++
Thread A reads counter (5), Thread B reads counter (5)
Both increment (6), both write (6) — Should be 7!

Always use synchronization when sharing data between threads!

phase3.prompt:
Which statement about multithreading is TRUE?

A) Adding more threads always makes a program faster
B) Threads share memory and can access the same data
C) A process cannot have multiple threads
D) All threads run in parallel on all CPUs
E) Threads never interact with each other

Select the CORRECT statement.
dialogueHints: Do threads share memory or have their own?
Is it always faster to have more threads?
phase3.openingDialogue: Multithreading — doing multiple things at once! 🧵
